text: No portions of this operation were edited out. It was played at four times the regular speed, however. The objective is to show that this approach does not add excessive operative time. This was performed in twenty-four minutes.
This patient had one previous operation for a perforated appendicitis approximately six years ago. The ventral incisional hernia was from the umbilical trocar site.
The surgeon removes omental adhesions from the anterior abdominal wall, demonstrating a clear separation between the omentum and the abdominal wall tissue.
The surgeon visually estimates the ventral incisional hernia to be approximately two centimeters in diameter.
A robotic approach may seem like overkill in this situation. However, the final repair is a superior result for many reasons.
The surgeon creates a peritoneal flap, revealing herniated preperitoneal adipose tissue protruding through the defect.
The patient may feel persistent pain or they may even notice a palpable bulge that resembles the previous hernia.
The surgeon exposes the full hernia defect and estimates its size, preparing to use a 6.4 cm piece of mesh. The preperitoneal pocket is extended circumferentially to accommodate the mesh placement.
The surgeon separates the peritoneal lining from the posterior rectus fascia, revealing the anatomical layers involved in the hernia repair.
The fascial layer is prepared to incorporate the mesh, ensuring a stronger and more reliable integration compared to fixation on the outside of the peritoneal layer.
The surgeon identifies the hernia sac as a small protrusion on the peritoneal layer, with the hernia contents visibly inverted back into the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon receives the suture material and a piece of mesh from the assistant, preparing to place the mesh into the preperitoneal pocket that has been extended circumferentially.
The surgeon closes the defect primarily using a running technique with an absorbable barbed suture, ensuring the edges of the tissue are approximated securely.
The goal is to separate the sutures by approximately one centimeter to disperse the tension over a larger space. Closing the hernia defect primarily reduces the risk of a seroma after this operation.
Seromas can be bothersome and can even become infected, compromising the integrity of the mesh.

I tend to prefer placing all sutures before tightening them down and closing the defect. I think it is helpful to keep the perspective of the defect in sight while placing sutures. However, this is simply a personal preference.

The surgeon adds four additional throws to the barbed suture to maintain tension, then revisits the suture line, ending at the center of the hernia. The final pass of the suture is incorporated into the mesh fixation.
This small step here mimics the utility and saves the cost of the Ventralite mesh with the echo positioning system.
The surgeon circumferentially sutures the mesh to the posterior fascial wall using an absorbable barbed suture, ensuring the mesh remains in apposition during the healing process.
The herniated contents are visibly reduced back into the abdominal space, demonstrating the effectiveness of the operative approach.

Patients appreciate a better result when this is done. Second, the hernia defect is closed primarily, preventing the hernia from becoming larger over time and reducing the potential for seroma formation.
The surgeon fixates the mesh to the robust rectus fascia using sutures, ensuring stability and preventing migration of the mesh.
The surgeon closes the peritoneal space over the mesh, effectively concealing it from the intestine to prevent adhesions and potential bowel obstructions.

A benefit that is not seen here is the location and the size of the incisions required for this operation. This is done through three separate eight millimeter incisions that are placed on the side of the abdominal wall, far away from the location of the mesh.
Mesh infections can be a very serious complication. Placing the incisions far away from the mesh reduces the risk that a skin infection would result in a mesh infection.
Incorporating the hernia sac in with the peritoneal closure is not necessary, but in some cases it may be helpful.
The surgeon removes all suture material from the abdomen, marking the completion of the operation.
Thank you for watching.
